content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 25, 1990


Clinton Pridmore
President
Napa County Resource Conservation District
General Engineering Contractors
1305 Capell Valley Road
Napa, CA 94558

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-90-519

Dear Mr. Pridmore:

This letter is in response to your request for confirmation
of telephone advice provided to you on July 31, 1990, regarding
your responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Your request for advice is
general in nature and does not concern a specific decision before
your agency. Consequently, we treat your letter as a request for
informal assistance pursuant to the provisions of Regulation
18329(c) (copy enclosed).²

QUESTION

Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prevent you
from working as the grading contractor in a parcel subject to ero-
sion control regulation by the Napa Valley Resource Conservation
District of which you are president?

CONCLUSION

The Act does not prevent you from working as the grading
contractor in a parcel subject to erosion control regulation by
the Napa Valley Resource Conservation District. However, in your
official capacity as president of the district, you must abstain

_______________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Sec-
tion 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

File No. I-90-519
Page 2

from participating in decisions which will have a material
financial effect on you, or on your business, or on any source of
income of $250 or more in the 12 months preceding the decision.
Additionally, the "nexus" provisions of the Act require you to
disqualify yourself from participating in decisions regarding erosion control in the particular parcel.

FACTS

On July 31, 1990, you telephoned this office for advice and
informed me that you are the elected President of the Napa Valley
Resource Conservation District (the "district"). You are also the
sole owner of an excavation and general contracting business.3
You wish to enter into a contract to provide your services as a
grading contractor for the Jim Silva subdivision parcel split in
Capel Valley. Your work will involve winterizing the land to
minimize damage from erosion. Work performed on the parcel is
subject to approval by the district.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. As president of the Napa County
Resource Conservation District you are a public official. (Section 82048.)

Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial
interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate
family or on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

***

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received
by or promised to the public official within 12
months prior to the time when the decision is made.

3 These facts include information you provided to me during our
telephone conversation of August 24, 1990.

File No. I-90-519
Page 3

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

***

For purposes of this section, indirect invest-
ment or interest means any investment or interest
owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public
official, by an agent on behalf of a public of-
ficial, or by a business entity or trust in which
the official's agents, spouse, and
dependent children own directly, indirectly, or
beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater.

You are the sole owner of an excavating and contracting busi-
ness. For purposes of our discussion we assume that your interest
in your company exceeds $1,000. Under these facts, you must
disqualify yourself from participating in any decision which would
foreseeably and materially affect you or your business. You are
also required to disqualify yourself from participation in any
decision which would foreseeably and materially affect any person
(including a business entity) which has been a source of income to
you in excess of $250 in the 12 months preceding the decision, or
affect any business entity in which you are a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee, or hold any position of management.
Disqualification is required whenever the decision will affect
these interests in a manner that is distinguishable from the ef-
fect on the public generally.

Foreseeability

The effects of a decision are reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that they will occur. To be foresee-
able, the effects of a decision must be more than a mere possibil-
ity; however certainty is not required. (Downey Care v. Downey
Community Development Com. (1987) 196 Cal. App. 3d 983, 989-991;
Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal. App. 3d 817; In re Thorner (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 198 (copy enclosed).) The Act seeks to prevent more
than actual conflicts of interest, it seeks to prevent even the
appearance of a possible conflict of interest. (Witt v. Morrow,
supra at 823.)

Your company is engaged in the business of erosion control.
When you complete an erosion control project, the district of
which you are president approves the work performed. The district
may deny approval and require additional work on the land. If it
is foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on you, on your business, or on any source of income of
$250 or more in the preceding 12 months, disqualification is
required.

File No. I-90-519
Page 4


Materiality

     To require disqualification, the foreseeable effect of a
decision on your financial interests must be material. The Com-
mission has adopted regulations which clarify when the effect of a
decision is material. When a source of income of $250 or more in
the 12 months preceding the decision is directly involved in a
decision before the official's agency, the official must
disqualify himself or herself from participating in the decision.
(Regulation 18702.1(a), copy enclosed.) A source of income is
directly involved in a decision before the public official's
agency when it:

     (1) Initiates the proceeding in which the
decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request or;

     (2) Is a named party in, or the subject of,
the proceeding concerning the decision before the
official or the official's agency.

     (3) A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person or business
entity.

                         Regulation 18702.1(b).

     Completion of the erosion control work is subject to district
approval. When the individual or business for whom you have
performed erosion control work and who has been a source of income
to you of $250 or more in the preceding 12 months comes before the
district for approval of the work, you must disqualify yourself
from participating in the decision.

     The decision to approve the erosion control work performed on
the property will also indirectly affect your business. This is
so because additional work may be required. When a business
entity in which a public official has an interest of more than
$1,000 is indirectly involved in a decision before the official's
agency, Regulation 18702.2 (copy enclosed) provides the necessary
guidelines to determine whether the decision is material. For a
relatively small business entity, the indirect effect of a deci-
sion is material if:

     (1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or

File No. I-90-519
Page 5

(2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or

(3) The decision will result in the increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.

Regulation 18702.2(g)

Thus, if the decision to approve the erosion control work you have
performed will have a material financial effect on your business,
your disqualification is required.

Nexus

Additionally, you are required to disqualify yourself if
there is a "nexus" between the purpose for which you receive
income in your private capacity and a governmental decision. A
nexus situation exists if you receive income for a purpose or
purpose which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by
the decision. (Regulation 18702.1(d).) Upon completion of the
erosion control work, the project will come before the district
for approval of the work performed. Under these circumstances,
there is a disqualifying nexus because you would be approving in
your official capacity the project for which you received income to
accomplish in your private capacity. Disqualification is thus
required.

We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Blanca M. Breeze
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:BMB:plh

Enclosures
